context: Rural government school in Andhra Pradesh. Students have completed the previous Agastya M1 sessions on Triangles, Quadrilaterals, and Circles. They know basic shapes and can measure with a ruler, but have not yet encountered formal area formulas. Most students think 'bigger looking' means 'bigger area' without a precise way to compare.
openingScene: You walk into the classroom carrying graph paper, a geoboard strung with nails, colourful rubber bands, cardboard cutouts of different shapes, and a coloured paper circle already divided into 16 sectors. You hold up two cardboard shapes — a long thin rectangle and a short wide square — and ask: which one is bigger? Priya immediately starts tracing the shapes with her finger, trying to compare them visually. Ravi folds his arms and says confidently that the longer one is obviously bigger. Lakshmi quietly places her notebook next to the rectangle, trying to measure it by eye. Arjun stretches a rubber band between his fingers and flicks it at his neighbour. Meena has already written 'Areas and Perimeters' in her notebook and is drawing both shapes carefully with her ruler.
